meta.title: Understanding ClaimFarm
meta.subtitle: From a farmer's photo to a filed crop-insurance claim
meta.promise: Learn how one photo becomes a filed insurance claim, and be able to explain every step with confidence.
outcomes: Explain what ClaimFarm does and the problem it solves, without notes
Trace one photo end to end, from webhook to a filed claim
Name each agent and the Qwen or Alibaba service it uses, and why
Answer a judge's hardest questions about limits and fraud
Point to the exact file behind any claim you make

c-story.name: What ClaimFarm is
c-story.summary: A WhatsApp/Telegram-to-claim autopilot for smallholder crop insurance.

c-problem.name: The problem and users
c-problem.summary: Fewer than 20% of eligible farmers file; the blockers are language, evidence, and literacy.

c-flow.name: End-to-end flow
c-flow.summary: Intake runs synchronously; similar-claims and fraud are computed at review time.

c-toolbox.name: Technology choices
c-toolbox.summary: Qwen trio for thinking, FastAPI, DashVector, OSS, Open-Meteo, Next.js, chosen for fit and cost.

c-architecture.name: Architecture and channels
c-architecture.summary: Signature-verified webhooks funnel every channel into one shared pipeline.

c-journey.name: The request journey
c-journey.summary: process_inbound assesses, corroborates, drafts, saves, and replies; an adjuster then approves.

c-agents.name: The agent pipeline
c-agents.summary: One focused agent per step, each returning structured, checkable output.

c-data.name: Data and state
c-data.summary: Three stores by shape: SQL records, OSS files, DashVector embeddings.

c-deploy.name: Running and deploying
c-deploy.summary: uv, Docker, Function Compute serverless, Vercel frontend, CI dual-push.

c-tradeoffs.name: Tradeoffs and the pitch
c-tradeoffs.summary: Honest, scoped limits each with a named production fix.

s-forensics-call.note: forensics imported lazily and run on the bytes

s-main-tg.note: Telegram secret check and inbound handler

s-arch.note: system map and data flow diagrams

s-docker.note: backend image; CMD execs uvicorn from the venv

s-web.note: Next.js adjuster console and marketing site

t-qwenvl.name: Qwen-VL-Max
t-qwenvl.purpose: Reads the crop photo and returns a structured damage report.
t-qwenvl.tradeoffs: Required by the hackathon; strong multimodal quality.

t-qwenmax.name: Qwen-Max
t-qwenmax.purpose: Drafts the claim and translates farmer replies.
t-qwenmax.tradeoffs: Cheaper than paying vision rates for text tasks.

t-embed.name: text-embedding-v3
t-embed.purpose: Turns claims into vectors for similarity and fraud.
t-embed.tradeoffs: One representation serves both RAG and fraud.

t-fastapi.name: FastAPI
t-fastapi.purpose: Receives webhooks and exposes the console JSON API.

t-dashvector.name: Alibaba DashVector
t-dashvector.purpose: Vector search for similar claims and agronomy notes.
t-dashvector.tradeoffs: Managed and Alibaba-aligned; Chroma fallback for dev.

t-oss.name: Alibaba OSS
t-oss.purpose: Stores photos and generated PDFs with signed URLs.
t-oss.tradeoffs: Keeps bytes out of the app process.

t-openmeteo.name: Open-Meteo
t-openmeteo.purpose: Historical weather to corroborate the damage claim.
t-openmeteo.tradeoffs: Free and keyless, removing a credential.

t-nextjs.name: Next.js 16
t-nextjs.purpose: Marketing site plus the adjuster console.

t-weasyprint.name: WeasyPrint
t-weasyprint.purpose: Renders the claim PDF from an HTML template.

t-fc.name: Function Compute 3.0
t-fc.tradeoffs: Cheap for bursty demo traffic; ephemeral /tmp.

g-agent.definition: A small piece of code that does one focused job by calling an AI model and tools, then returns a structured result.

g-pipeline.definition: A chain of steps where each step's output feeds the next.

g-multimodal.definition: An AI model that understands more than text. Qwen-VL-Max can read images.

g-embedding.definition: A list of numbers capturing meaning, so similar things sit close together.

g-vectordb.definition: A store that searches by meaning using embeddings. Here, DashVector.

g-rag.definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: fetch relevant facts and feed them to the model so its answer is grounded.

g-webhook.definition: A URL a service calls when something happens. Telegram calls ClaimFarm when a farmer sends a message.

g-hitl.definition: The system prepares the work but a person makes the final call.

g-fc.definition: Alibaba's serverless runtime; runs a container that scales to zero when idle.

g-oss.definition: Alibaba Object Storage Service: cloud file storage for photos and PDFs.

g-exif.definition: Hidden metadata inside a photo (camera, date, sometimes GPS). Forensics checks it to spot fakes.

g-idempotent.definition: Safe to run more than once with the same result. Matters because a webhook can be delivered twice.

sequenceDiagram

  participant VL as Qwen-VL-Max

  CH->>P: verified payload

d-pipeline.title: The agent pipeline
d-pipeline.code:
flowchart TD
  IN[Inbound photo] --> LANG[detect_language]
  LANG --> DMG[assess_damage]
  DMG --> FOR[photo_forensics]
  FOR --> WX[corroborate]
  WX --> DRAFT[build_claim]
  DRAFT --> PDF[render_claim_pdf]
  PDF --> SAVE[save + index]
  SAVE --> REPLY[localized reply]
  SAVE --> REVIEW[Adjuster console]
  REVIEW --> RAGF[find_similar + fraud_check]

children.name: agents
children.role: one focused AI step per file

modules.title: The five-minute story
modules.summary: What ClaimFarm is, in one breath.
modules.milestone: You can pitch ClaimFarm in one sentence.

lessons.title: What is ClaimFarm?

levels.eli10: Imagine a farmer whose field gets flooded. Normally they must fill scary paper forms in a language they may not read. ClaimFarm lets them just take a photo and send it like a text. A smart computer looks at the photo, checks the weather, writes the form, and a person says yes or no. Then the farmer gets paid, with a message in their own language.
levels.beginner: ClaimFarm turns a single crop photo sent over Telegram or WhatsApp into a filed crop-insurance claim. AI reads the photo, cross-checks the weather, drafts the claim as a PDF, and a human adjuster approves it. It was built for the Qwen Cloud hackathon, Track 4 (Autopilot Agent).
levels.intermediate: ClaimFarm is a multimodal agent pipeline. A messaging webhook hits a FastAPI backend that runs ordered agents (vision, forensics, weather, drafting, multilingual), persists a Claim, and surfaces it in a Next.js console for human approval, which then files to an insurer API. Backend on Alibaba Function Compute; AI on Qwen Cloud.
levels.advanced: It is an ambiguous-input autopilot agent with tool calls and a human checkpoint, exactly the Track 4 brief. The orchestrator is a deterministic Python sequence rather than an LLM planner, which keeps latency and failure modes bounded inside a serverless webhook timeout while still doing real multimodal reasoning per step.

facets.what: A WhatsApp/Telegram-to-claim autopilot for smallholder crop insurance.
facets.why: To collapse a multi-page, multi-language, evidence-heavy form into one photo.
facets.how: A webhook runs an agent pipeline that assesses, corroborates, drafts, and routes a claim to a human.
facets.whatif: Remove the human approval step and it becomes automatic payouts, which insurers will not accept and fraud would exploit.

lessons.analogy: It is like a tax-filing app that does your whole return from one receipt photo, but a real accountant still signs off before it is submitted.
lessons.insight: The orchestrator is plain Python, not an LLM agent loop. A fixed sequence beats a free-form planner for a webhook that must answer fast.

flow.action: sends a damaged-crop photo to the bot
assesses, corroborates, and drafts a claim
is notified in their own language

quiz.q: What is the one input a farmer must provide to start a claim?
options: A photo of the damaged crop
quiz.hint: The pitch is about collapsing the workflow to one thing.
quiz.why: A single photo (optionally with a few words in any language) is the entry point; everything else is generated.

quiz.q: Where does a human still make a decision?
options: An adjuster approves before the claim is filed
The farmer approves the AI wording
quiz.hint: Track 4: human-in-the-loop checkpoint.
quiz.why: An adjuster reviews the AI assessment, weather, and fraud flags, then approves. That checkpoint is core.

lessons.teachBack: In two sentences, explain ClaimFarm to a non-technical friend without using the word 'AI'.

modules.title: The problem being solved
modules.summary: Why this is worth building and who uses it.

lessons.title: Why most farmers never file a claim

levels.beginner: Around 500 million smallholder farmers face crop loss, but fewer than 20% of eligible farmers ever file a claim. The blockers are language, the demand for structured evidence, and assumed domain literacy. ClaimFarm targets exactly those: any language in, evidence generated from a photo, no forms to understand.
levels.intermediate: The users are two-sided. Farmers interact only through a chat channel in their own language. Adjusters and insurers use the Next.js console to review AI-assembled evidence. The product is a real SaaS surface (auth, RBAC, billing and identity abstractions), not just a demo script.
levels.advanced: The wedge is conversion rate: lowering the activation cost of filing from hours of literate, structured effort to one photo. That reframes the metric from 'AI accuracy' to 'percentage of eligible losses that become reviewed claims', which is why the human checkpoint and multilingual reply matter as much as the vision model.

facets.what: A conversion problem: eligible losses that never become filed claims.
facets.why: The value is unlocking claims that currently never happen, not shaving minutes off existing ones.
facets.how: By removing the three blockers (language, evidence, literacy) at the point of contact.
facets.whatif: If the target user were a large insured agribusiness, the photo-first chat interface would be the wrong tool.

lessons.example: A farmer who reads only Telugu and has no scanner can now produce a complete, evidence-backed claim from a phone photo.
lessons.analogy: The difference between a tax form mailed to your house and a one-tap 'snap your W-2' app: same outcome, far more people finish.

quiz.q: What metric best captures ClaimFarm's value?
options: Percentage of eligible losses that become reviewed claims
quiz.hint: What was failing before: people not filing at all.
quiz.why: The point is conversion: turning losses that never got filed into reviewed claims.

lessons.teachBack: Explain why 'fewer than 20% file a claim' makes this a conversion problem, not an accuracy problem.

modules.title: What the system does
modules.summary: The eight steps from photo to payout.

lessons.title: The eight steps, and the two triggers

levels.beginner: End to end: (1) farmer sends a photo, (2) Qwen-VL assesses crop, cause, severity, (3) forensics checks the photo is real, (4) Open-Meteo weather is cross-referenced, (5) embeddings + DashVector pull similar past claims, (6) Qwen-Max drafts a claim PDF, (7) it lands in the adjuster console, (8) on approval it is filed and the farmer is notified.
levels.intermediate: The intake half (steps 1-6 plus the reply) runs synchronously in process_inbound. The review half (similar + fraud) is assembled on demand by api_claims.get_claim when an adjuster opens the claim, and post_decision handles approval. So the eight steps split across two trigger points.
levels.advanced: Splitting work across the webhook (write path) and the console fetch (read path) is deliberate: heavy similarity and fraud computation does not need to run inside the farmer-facing webhook, so it is deferred to get_claim. This keeps reply latency low and makes retrieval lazy and cache-friendly.

facets.what: A two-phase flow: synchronous intake, then on-demand review assembly.
facets.why: To answer the farmer fast while still doing deep analysis for the adjuster.
facets.how: process_inbound does steps 1-6 and replies; get_claim adds similar-claims and fraud at review time.
facets.whatif: Move similar/fraud into the webhook and the farmer waits longer for no benefit to them.

lessons.insight: The farmer gets a reply in seconds, but the 'similar past claims' panel is only computed when the adjuster opens the claim.
lessons.analogy: A restaurant plates your food fast (intake), but the detailed cost report is run later by the manager (review), not while you wait.

quiz.q: Where do 'similar past claims' and fraud flags get computed?
options: In api_claims.get_claim, when an adjuster opens the claim
quiz.hint: Who needs it: the farmer or the adjuster?
quiz.why: find_similar and fraud_check.check run in get_claim at review time, keeping the reply fast.

lessons.teachBack: Explain why steps 1-6 are synchronous but similar-claims and fraud are deferred.

lessons.title: What each technology is for, and why

levels.beginner: The Qwen trio does the thinking: Qwen-VL-Max sees photos, Qwen-Max writes and translates, text-embedding-v3 turns text into numbers for similarity. FastAPI is the web server. DashVector is the meaning-search database (Chroma locally). OSS stores files. Open-Meteo gives weather. WeasyPrint makes the PDF. Next.js builds the console.
levels.intermediate: Qwen Cloud is reached through the OpenAI SDK because DashScope-International is OpenAI-compatible. Models are named in config: qwen-vl-max, qwen-max, text-embedding-v3. DashVector is the prod vector store with a Chroma fallback selected by VECTOR_STORE.
levels.advanced: Vision and reasoning are split across two models so the cheaper text model handles drafting/translation rather than paying VL rates; embeddings power both RAG and fraud-similarity from one representation; the OpenAI-compatible surface makes the whole stack provider-swappable; OSS signed URLs avoid serving bytes through the app; Open-Meteo is keyless, removing a credential and a cost.

facets.what: A set of single-purpose tools wired together by thin client wrappers.
facets.why: Hackathon rules required Alibaba Cloud + Qwen; the rest were chosen for fit and cost.
facets.how: app/clients/* wrap each service so the agents stay clean and providers are swappable.
facets.whatif: Swap qwen.py's base_url and models and the same agents run on another OpenAI-compatible provider.

lessons.insight: claim_drafter uses qwen-max (cheap text) while damage_assessor uses qwen-vl-max (vision). Using VL for drafting would cost more for no benefit.

quiz.q: Why can ClaimFarm use the OpenAI SDK to call Qwen?
options: Qwen Cloud (DashScope) exposes an OpenAI-compatible API
It does not; that is a bug
quiz.hint: See the comment next to the openai dependency.
quiz.why: DashScope-International is OpenAI-compatible, so the openai client works by changing base_url and model names.

quiz.q: Why use qwen-max for drafting but qwen-vl-max for the photo?
options: Vision tokens are pricier; only the photo step needs vision
quiz.hint: Think cost per task.
quiz.why: Only the photo step needs multimodal vision; drafting is text-only, so the cheaper text model fits.

lessons.teachBack: Explain the benefit of wrapping every external service in app/clients/ instead of calling SDKs from the agents.

modules.title: Architecture and component map
modules.summary: How channels, backend, agents, and console connect.
modules.milestone: You can draw the system on a whiteboard from memory.

lessons.title: From a message to the backend

levels.beginner: Messaging providers (Telegram, Twilio for WhatsApp, Bird) each call a webhook URL on the FastAPI backend. main.py verifies the request is genuine, then hands the payload to the pipeline. All three channels funnel into the same process_inbound logic, so the agents do not care which app the farmer used.
levels.intermediate: main.py registers three webhook routes. Twilio is verified with HMAC-SHA1 over url+sorted params; Bird with HMAC-SHA256 over the body; Telegram with a secret token header. Unconfigured secrets cause the webhook to reject, not open (a documented fail-closed choice).
levels.advanced: The channel adapters normalize provider payloads into one internal shape, an anti-corruption layer that isolates provider quirks. Webhooks run the pipeline in a BackgroundTask so the 200 returns within the provider timeout, and signature verification is the trust boundary for the whole system.

facets.what: A webhook front door with per-provider verification feeding one shared pipeline.
facets.why: So any messaging channel can be added without touching the agents.
facets.how: main.py verifies the caller, normalizes the payload, and runs process_inbound.
facets.whatif: Skip signature verification and anyone could POST fake claims into your queue.

lessons.warning: An unconfigured webhook secret makes the endpoint reject requests (fail-closed), so a misconfiguration can never silently open the door to forged claims.
lessons.analogy: Three couriers all drop parcels through one mail slot; the sorting room inside works the same regardless of courier.

flow.action: normalizes it to the internal shape
runs the shared pipeline in a BackgroundTask

quiz.q: What happens if a channel's webhook secret is not configured?
quiz.hint: Look for the SEC-008 log lines.
quiz.why: An unconfigured webhook is treated as closed and rejects, so a misconfiguration never opens the door to forged claims.

quiz.q: Why do all three channels share process_inbound?
options: So the pipeline is independent of the messaging provider
quiz.hint: What stays stable when you add a channel?
quiz.why: Normalizing every provider into one internal shape means the agents never change when a channel is added.

lessons.teachBack: Explain the fail-closed webhook decision and why it matters for a system that creates insurance claims.

modules.title: A complete journey
modules.summary: Trace one photo to a filed, approved claim.

lessons.title: Inside process_inbound

levels.beginner: process_inbound is the recipe. If there is no photo, it asks for one. Otherwise it downloads the image, detects the language, calls assess_damage, corroborates with weather, builds a Claim, renders a PDF, saves it, and sends a localized reply. If saving fails it still replies, so the farmer is never left hanging.
levels.intermediate: It base64-encodes the photo into a data URL for Qwen-VL, builds a Farmer (using a default GPS for the demo), calls build_claim, then render_claim_pdf, persists via claims_repo.save, sets ClaimStatus.pending_review, and replies. Note the try/except around persistence that still saves a bare claim and still replies on failure.
levels.advanced: The function is a linear orchestration with defensive fallbacks rather than transactional rigor: persistence failure degrades to a best-effort save plus a guaranteed reply, prioritizing farmer experience over strict consistency. The default GPS constant is a demo shortcut and an explicit limitation.

facets.what: The synchronous orchestrator that turns one inbound photo into a saved, pending claim.
facets.why: It is the single place the whole intake sequence lives, in order.
facets.how: Download, detect language, assess, corroborate, draft, render, save, reply.
facets.whatif: Reorder corroborate before assess_damage and the weather step has no damage cause to check against.

lessons.example: No photo in the message? process_inbound short-circuits with 'Please send a photo...' and returns awaiting_photo, never running the AI.
lessons.warning: It uses a default GPS when the farmer does not share location. A real deployment needs an opt-in location share; the pipeline already accepts lat/lon.

flow.action: fetches the photo bytes from the channel
guesses the farmer's language from any caption
asks Qwen-VL for crop, cause, severity as JSON
cross-checks Open-Meteo weather for that date and place
drafts the claim, then render_claim_pdf makes the PDF
persists it and indexes an embedding in DashVector
replies to the farmer in their language

quiz.q: What does process_inbound do if saving the claim fails?
options: Crashes and the farmer hears nothing
Logs it, saves a bare claim, and still replies
quiz.hint: Look at the try/except around claims_repo.save.
quiz.why: It catches the failure, falls back to a best-effort save, and still replies. Farmer experience beats strict consistency.

quiz.q: Why is the photo turned into a base64 data URL?
options: So Qwen-VL can receive the image inline as an image_url part
quiz.hint: See how vision_chat passes the image.
quiz.why: Qwen-VL accepts a data URL as an image_url content part, so the bytes are encoded inline for the vision call.

lessons.teachBack: Narrate process_inbound out loud as if demoing, naming each step and what it produces.

lessons.title: The adjuster decision

levels.beginner: The Next.js console calls the backend's JSON API. get_claim returns the claim plus the top-3 similar past claims and any fraud flags, so the adjuster sees evidence in one view. When they approve, post_decision records it, files to the insurer, and notifies the farmer in their language.
levels.intermediate: install_api wires read endpoints (list_claims, get_claim, get_claim_photo, get_localized_reply) and the write endpoint post_decision. get_claim calls past_claim_rag.find_similar(k=3) and fraud_check.check on demand. post_decision flips status, calls the insurer client, and _notify_farmer sends the localized message.
levels.advanced: This is the human-in-the-loop boundary and the read/write split: the console reads an assembled evidence bundle and writes exactly one decision. Lazy similarity/fraud in get_claim keeps intake cheap and makes retrieval a function of adjuster attention, not message volume.

lessons.example: Opening claim CF-123 triggers a DashVector lookup for 3 similar claims and a fraud scan, returned in one get_claim response.
lessons.analogy: A loan officer's dashboard: it gathers your history and red flags into one view so they can make a single yes/no call.
lessons.insight: The human approval step is the feature, not a limitation, when pitching to an insurer.

quiz.q: What does get_claim add on top of the stored claim?
options: Top-3 similar past claims and fraud flags
quiz.hint: Two agent calls happen inside get_claim.
quiz.why: get_claim calls find_similar(k=3) and fraud_check.check, bundling evidence for the adjuster.

lessons.teachBack: Explain why the human approval step is a strength when pitching to an insurer.

modules.title: The agent pipeline up close
modules.summary: What each agent does and how it stays reliable.
modules.milestone: You can name every agent and its model.

lessons.title: Seeing the photo: damage and forensics

levels.beginner: Two helpers look at the photo. One says what is wrong with the crop. The other checks whether the photo is honest, not downloaded from the internet or made by a computer.
levels.intermediate: damage_assessor sends the photo to Qwen-VL with a strict prompt asking for JSON: crop_type, damage_cause, severity, affected_area_pct, confidence, indicators. photo_forensics checks authenticity using EXIF, a VL authenticity prompt, and perceptual hashing.
levels.advanced: Structured JSON output plus a conservative system prompt turns a free-form model into a typed function returning DamageAssessment, so downstream code can branch on confidence. Forensics is defense-in-depth: EXIF, perceptual hash, and a VL authenticity prompt each cover a different attack, none trusted alone.

facets.what: The vision layer: a typed damage report plus an authenticity check.
facets.why: Everything downstream depends on a trustworthy read of the photo.
facets.how: Qwen-VL with a strict JSON prompt for damage; EXIF + hash + VL for forensics.
facets.whatif: Drop json_mode and the strict prompt, and downstream parsing into DamageAssessment becomes fragile.

lessons.example: Given a screenshot of a stock photo, forensics can flag it via EXIF absence and a perceptual-hash match even if the crop looks real.
lessons.analogy: A doctor reads the X-ray (damage), while a lab tech confirms the X-ray is actually yours (forensics).

walkthrough.code:
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """\
You are a crop insurance damage assessor.
Identify the crop, whether damage is present, the primary cause,
a severity score (0-100), affected area %, and your confidence.
Be conservative. If unsure, say so via low confidence.
Return STRICT JSON only.
"""
walkthrough.note: The prompt forbids invented symptoms and demands JSON-only output.
The photo is passed as an image_url content part, the same shape the OpenAI SDK uses.

quiz.q: How does ClaimFarm make Qwen-VL's output safe to use in code?
options: It requests strict JSON and parses it into a DamageAssessment model
It runs OCR on the reply
quiz.hint: Look at json_mode and the model the result becomes.
quiz.why: A strict JSON prompt plus json_mode yields a typed DamageAssessment, a reliable typed function.

quiz.q: Why does forensics use three different checks?
options: Defense in depth: EXIF, perceptual hash, and a VL authenticity prompt each catch a different fake
quiz.hint: Each check covers a different attack.
quiz.why: EXIF, perceptual hashing, and a VL authenticity prompt each detect a different manipulation; none is trusted alone.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how strict JSON output makes an LLM behave like a normal typed function.

lessons.title: Corroborating: weather, RAG, and fraud

levels.beginner: Before trusting the claim, ClaimFarm double-checks. It looks up whether the weather that day matches the damage, finds past claims that look the same, and watches for tricks like the same photo used twice.
levels.intermediate: weather_corroborator pulls Open-Meteo history for the location and date and uses Qwen-Max to judge consistency. past_claim_rag embeds the claim and searches DashVector for similar priors; new claims are indexed on save. fraud_check returns flags with similarity scores.
levels.advanced: One embedding representation serves two purposes: helpfulness (retrieve precedents) and risk (detect near-duplicate claims). Weather corroboration grounds the vision verdict in an independent, keyless source. Fraud is similarity-based, so it generalizes, at the cost of needing a populated past_claims collection.

facets.what: The corroboration layer: independent checks on the AI's verdict.
facets.why: To ground a single model's read in weather data and historical precedent.
facets.how: Open-Meteo for weather; embeddings + DashVector for similar claims and fraud.
facets.whatif: Empty the past_claims collection and fraud-by-similarity has nothing to compare against.

lessons.insight: The same embedding similarity surfaces useful precedents for the adjuster and suspicious near-duplicates for fraud. One representation, two jobs.
lessons.example: A 'drought' verdict is strengthened when Open-Meteo shows weeks of no rain at that GPS and date; weakened if it shows heavy rainfall.

quiz.q: How does one embedding model serve both RAG and fraud detection?
options: Similar claims are 'helpful precedents' and 'near-duplicate risk' from the same similarity search
quiz.hint: Same vectors, two interpretations.
quiz.why: The same similarity surfaces useful precedents and suspicious near-duplicates, so one representation powers both.

lessons.teachBack: Explain why grounding the vision verdict in Open-Meteo weather reduces risk.

lessons.title: Drafting and speaking the farmer's language

levels.beginner: Now ClaimFarm writes the form using everything it learned, turns it into a neat PDF, and talks back to the farmer in their own language.
levels.intermediate: claim_drafter.build_claim assembles the Claim; render_claim_pdf makes a PDF with WeasyPrint and Jinja2. multilingual.detect_language finds the language, and status_message/localize produce replies in that language using Qwen-Max.
levels.advanced: PDF generation via HTML+CSS keeps the document templated and restyleable, and the artifact is stored in OSS for durable signed access. Translating templated status strings (rather than free-generating replies) bounds what the model can say to the farmer, important for a regulated message that must not overpromise a payout.

facets.what: The output layer: a PDF claim plus a localized human reply.
facets.why: The farmer needs an understandable response; the insurer needs a document.
facets.how: Qwen-Max drafts/translates; WeasyPrint renders the PDF from an HTML template.
facets.whatif: Free-generate replies instead of translating templates and the bot might promise a payout it cannot guarantee.

lessons.warning: Farmer-facing replies translate fixed templates, not free text, so the bot never accidentally promises a payout.
lessons.example: A Hindi-speaking farmer gets the same 'claim started, under review' status the system always sends, but in Hindi.

quiz.q: Why use templated, translated status messages instead of free-form AI replies?
options: To bound what the bot can say so it never overpromises a payout
quiz.hint: What could a wrong promise to a farmer cost?
quiz.why: Translating fixed templates keeps farmer-facing copy controlled, avoiding accidental promises in a regulated context.

lessons.teachBack: Explain when ClaimFarm uses Qwen-VL vs Qwen-Max vs the embedding model, with one example each.

modules.title: Data, running it, and tradeoffs
modules.summary: State, deployment, limits, and the pitch.
modules.milestone: You can answer a judge's hardest question.

lessons.title: Data and state: what is stored where

levels.beginner: ClaimFarm remembers three kinds of things. Small facts (claims, farmers) go in a simple database. Big files (photos, PDFs) go in a cloud locker. And 'meaning' data for search goes in a vector database.
levels.intermediate: Structured data lives in SQL via SQLModel (SQLite by default). Files live in Alibaba OSS. Embeddings live in DashVector (collections past_claims and agronomy_kb). Core shapes: Claim, Farmer, DamageAssessment.
levels.advanced: Three stores by data shape: relational for records, object storage for blobs, vector for semantic search. On Function Compute the SQLite file lands in ephemeral /tmp, hence the startup demo-seed and the documented path to Postgres for durability.

facets.what: Three stores chosen by data shape: SQL, object storage, and vector.
facets.why: Records, files, and semantic search have different access patterns.
facets.how: SQLModel for claims/users, OSS for photos/PDFs, DashVector for embeddings.
facets.whatif: Keep SQLite on Function Compute for production and data vanishes when the instance recycles.

lessons.warning: On Function Compute the SQLite file is in ephemeral /tmp and is wiped when the instance recycles; production uses Postgres via DATABASE_URL.
lessons.analogy: An office: filing cabinet for forms (SQL), storage room for boxes (OSS), and a card catalog that finds things by topic (vector DB).

quiz.q: Why move from SQLite to Postgres for production?
options: On Function Compute the SQLite file is in ephemeral /tmp and is wiped on recycle
quiz.hint: Recall why there is a startup demo-seed.
quiz.why: FC's /tmp is ephemeral, so SQLite data does not survive; Postgres via DATABASE_URL is durable.

lessons.teachBack: Explain why ClaimFarm uses three kinds of storage instead of one database.

lessons.title: Running, testing, and deploying it

levels.beginner: To run ClaimFarm you install its parts, give it secret keys, and start it. To put it online it is packed into a container and shipped to Alibaba's cloud, where it wakes up only when needed.
levels.intermediate: Locally: install deps with uv, copy .env.example to .env and fill keys, run FastAPI and the Next.js app, run pytest. Deploy: build the Docker image and run it on Alibaba Function Compute (scale-to-zero), frontend on Vercel. GitHub Actions lints and tests on every push.
levels.advanced: Two quirks matter for a confident demo: the Dockerfile CMD execs uvicorn directly from the venv (using 'uv run' would re-sync at startup), and pyproject keeps license as the SPDX string 'MIT' (the file-reference form fails when LICENSE is absent from the FC image path). FC pulls from ghcr.io because the ACR Economy tier is not pullable.

facets.what: A uv + Docker + Function Compute workflow with CI and a Vercel frontend.
facets.why: Serverless scale-to-zero fits bursty, demo-friendly, low-cost hosting.
facets.how: uv installs, pytest tests, Docker builds, FC runs the container, Vercel serves the console.
facets.whatif: Use 'uv run' in the Dockerfile CMD and startup re-syncs metadata, slowing cold starts.

lessons.experiment: From a fresh clone: uv sync, cp .env.example .env and fill keys, run uvicorn, then pytest. You now have the backend running locally.

quiz.q: Why does the Dockerfile CMD exec uvicorn directly instead of 'uv run'?
options: 'uv run' re-syncs the project at startup, slowing cold starts
quiz.hint: What happens at container startup?
quiz.why: 'uv run' re-resolves and re-syncs metadata at startup. Calling the venv's uvicorn directly avoids that on every cold start.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how a commit to main becomes a running container on Function Compute.

lessons.title: Tradeoffs, limits, and explaining it to a judge

levels.beginner: Nothing is perfect. ClaimFarm makes a few honest shortcuts for the demo, like guessing the location when the farmer does not share it. Knowing these makes you sound trustworthy when you show it off.
levels.intermediate: Be honest about the seams: default GPS, ephemeral SQLite (hence the seed), WhatsApp trial tiers so the live demo uses Telegram, and a mock insurer. These are deliberate, documented choices with clear production paths.
levels.advanced: Frame limits as scoping decisions with named upgrades. Pre-empting the obvious judge questions with the upgrade path demonstrates production thinking, which is exactly what Track 4 is judged on.

facets.what: An honest map of what is real, what is scoped, and the upgrade path.
facets.why: Confident, truthful framing wins judges and survives hard questions.
facets.how: Lead with the real SaaS surface and the human checkpoint; name each limit with its fix.
facets.whatif: Hide the limits and one pointed question can unravel the whole pitch.

rows.b: Opt-in location share at registration; pipeline already takes lat/lon

lessons.insight: Judge: 'How do you know the farmer's location?' You: 'For the demo we default it; production requires an opt-in share. The pipeline already takes lat/lon.'
lessons.example: Lead with the real, sellable SaaS surface: auth, RBAC, audit, and a genuine human-in-the-loop, then be candid about the scoped limits.

quiz.q: Best way to handle the 'default GPS' limitation in a pitch?
options: State it as a demo shortcut and name the fix (opt-in share); note the pipeline already accepts lat/lon
quiz.hint: Honesty plus a concrete upgrade path.
quiz.why: Naming the limit and its fix shows production thinking and builds trust, which Track 4 rewards.

quiz.q: Which is a genuine strength to lead with?
options: A real two-sided SaaS surface plus a true human-in-the-loop
quiz.hint: What is built beyond a demo script?
quiz.why: The real product surface and the human approval step are the differentiators; lead with them, then be candid.

lessons.teachBack: Give your full 60-second judge pitch: what it is, the problem, the live demo path, the human checkpoint, and one honest limit with its fix.
lessons.deeper: Re-read docs/security.md before a demo so you can answer 'how do you stop fraud and forged photos' with specifics: signature-verified webhooks, defense-in-depth forensics, and similarity-based fraud flags reviewed by a human.

modules.title: Teach it back
modules.summary: Prove you can explain ClaimFarm end to end.
modules.milestone: You can teach ClaimFarm to someone else.

lessons.title: The full walkthrough, in your words

levels.beginner: Time to be the teacher. Tell the whole story of one photo becoming a paid claim, in your own words, like explaining it to a friend.
levels.intermediate: Aim for a 3-5 minute spoken walkthrough that names real files: main.py (webhook + verify), whatsapp_intake.process_inbound (intake), the agents, api_claims (review + post_decision), and the three stores. Close with one limit and its fix.
levels.advanced: Mastery means switching altitude on demand: the 30-second pitch, then zoom to a specific file when a judge probes, then back to the business framing, all while staying truthful about what is demo-scoped.

lessons.example: 'A farmer sends a photo to our Telegram bot. main.py verifies the webhook and hands it to process_inbound, which...' through to 'an adjuster approves in the console and post_decision files it.'

quiz.q: A strong end-to-end explanation should include all of these EXCEPT:
options: Naming a real file or two
An honest limitation and its fix
Claiming it is fully automatic with no humans
quiz.hint: One contradicts the actual design.
quiz.why: ClaimFarm is explicitly human-in-the-loop. Claiming full automation is untrue and weaker for an insurance pitch.

lessons.teachBack: Write your final 3-minute ClaimFarm walkthrough, citing at least three real files and ending with one honest limitation and its fix.
